coredata.dc:title: Enhancing the Efficiency of DSSCs by the Modification of TiO2 Photoanodes using N, F and S, co-doped Graphene Quantum Dots
coredata.dc:creator: Kundu, Sumana
Sarojinijeeva, P.
Karthick, R.
Anantharaj, G.
Saritha, G.
Bera, Rajesh
Anandan, S.
Patra, Amitava
Ragupathy, P.
Selvaraj, M.
Jeyakumar, D.
Pillai, K. Vijayamohanan
coredata.dc:description: We report an enhanced power conversion efficiency (PCE) of 11.7%±0.2 and a fill factor (FF) of 71% for dye-sensitized solar cells (DSSC) with an active area of 0.16cm2 after modifying the TiO2photoanode with size-selective (ca. 2nm)N,F,S-codoped graphene quantum dots (NFS-GQDs) that exhibit a photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) of 70%. An upward shift in the Fermi level has been observed, perhaps responsible for the improved performance along with the possibility of preventing the back electron transfer from TiO2. Mott Schottky analysis indicates a shift (52mV)in the flat band potential, which is directly related to the Voc of the system. Detailed characterization (IPCE, TCSPC etc) indicates the important role of hetero atoms in facilitating the enhanced performance. Thus, our results suggest that the incorporation of size controlled, hetero atom doped GQDs can enhance the efficiency of DSSCs enabling more opto-electronic applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Dye sensitised solar cell (DSSC)
Photoluminiscence quantum yield (PLQY)
Power conversion efficiency (PCE)
Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy (EIS)
Graphene Quantum Dots (GQDs)
Incident photon-to-current efficiency (IPCE)
Time-correlated single-photon counting (TCSPC)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A schematic representation of the NFS-GQD modified anode showing the Fermi level change (an upward 52 mV shift in the flat band potential compared to bare TiO2) due to modification where CB indicates conduction band and VB indicates valence band.
HRTEM (a) low and (b) high magnification image of NFS-GQDs incorporated on rutile TiO2 (c) high magnification image of NFS-GQDs incorporated on anatase TiO2.

(a) A comparison of the UV-VIS- Diffuse Reflectance Spectra (UV-VIS-DRS) of TiO2, NFS-GQDs-TiO2 with and without dye (b) UV–vis spectra of N719 dye with and without doped GQDs (c) Comparison of PL spectra of NFS-GQDs, TiO2 with and without NFS-GQDs.

(a) Variation of I–V characteristics of only TiO2 with dye which shows η=7.48% (FF=63%) (i), NFS-GQDs-TiO2-N719 dye with respect to different doped GQDs loading corresponding to different time interval (3h: η=9.7% (FF=71.70%) (ii), 8h: η=11.7% (FF=71%) (iii), and 12h: η=5.4% (FF=50%) (iv)) (b) Comparison of EIS of TiO2, NFS-GQDs-TiO2 at three different dipping time (3, 8 and 12h) (c) Bode plot of the respective EIS and (d) Mott-Schottky analysis of the TiO2, NFS-GQDs-TiO2 at 3, 8 and 12h under forward bias under dark condition.

(a) Comparision of Incident photon-to-current efficiency (IPCE) spectra of the DSSC with and without NFS-GQDs along with their Jsc plot obtained from IPCE. (b) respective integral Jsc plot obtained from IPCE.

row.entry:
Life time (mS)
(from Nyquist plot Bisquert element fitting)

head.ce:title: Enhancing the Efficiency of DSSCs by the Modification of TiO2 Photoanodes using N, F and S, co-doped Graphene Quantum Dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: CSIR-Central Electrochemical Research Institute (CSIR-CECRI), Karaikudi 630003, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: CSIR-Network of Institutes for Solar Energy, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: CSIR-Network of Institutes for Solar Energy

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Academy of Scientific & Innovative Research (AcSIR), Chennai, 600113, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Nanomaterials and Solar Energy Conversion Lab, Department of Chemistry, National Institute of Technology, Tiruchirappalli, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Nanomaterials and Solar Energy Conversion Lab

ce:affiliation.ce:label: e
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Materials Science, Indian Association for the Cultivation of Science, Kolkata 700 032, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Indian Association for the Cultivation of Science

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: CSIR-Central Electrochemical Research Institute (CSIR-CECRI), Karaikudi 630003, India.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We report an enhanced power conversion efficiency (PCE) of 11.7%±0.2 and a fill factor (FF) of 71% for dye-sensitized solar cells (DSSC) with an active area of 0.16cm2 after modifying the TiO2photoanode with size-selective (ca. 2nm)N,F,S-codoped graphene quantum dots (NFS-GQDs) that exhibit a photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) of 70%. An upward shift in the Fermi level has been observed, perhaps responsible for the improved performance along with the possibility of preventing the back electron transfer from TiO2. Mott Schottky analysis indicates a shift (52mV)in the flat band potential, which is directly related to the Voc of the system. Detailed characterization (IPCE, TCSPC etc) indicates the important role of hetero atoms in facilitating the enhanced performance. Thus, our results suggest that the incorporation of size controlled, hetero atom doped GQDs can enhance the efficiency of DSSCs enabling more opto-electronic applications.

ce:section.ce:para: The ever growing appetite for energy demand with respect to the expanding world population and modernization has brought several daunting challenges to mankind due to the relentless usage of conventional energy sources [1]. Some of these could be solved by utilizing more renewable resources like solar and wind and the availability of many new materials has helped to increase the pace of deployment. Among the various solar energy systems investigated over the years, conventional silicon solar cells have received much attention due their high efficiency. However, high manufacturing cost and its inadequate performance under diffused sunlight hinder their widespread utilization. Third generation solar cells potentially replace the conventional silicon cells with their low processing cost, ease of fabrication, moderate efficiency and better performance during cloudy days. Of late, Dye-sensitized solar cells (DSSCs) have grabbed more attention in this category. DSSC works under diffused sunlight mainly because the semiconductor acts as charge carrier and dye as photon collector, whereas in silicon solar cells, silicon acts as both the charge transport carrier as well as the source for photo-electrons. Dye molecules are sensitive enough to collect the photons under diffused sunlight thus paving the way for this crucial difference [2].

There are several ways of modifying DSSC in order to improve the power conversion efficiency (PCE). Photoanode modification is one of the important methods in addition to designing new redox couple with lower potential, molecular engineering of the dye and the alteration in the counter electrode composition especially using inexpensive and efficient materials as substitutes for Pt [3]. Since the photoanode in DSSC acts as light harvesting part as well as charge carrier its modification could, in principle, play a crucial role in enhancing the PCE contribution. Commonly used photoanode is TiO2 due to its relatively high energy conversion efficiency, high specific surface area, non-toxicity and low cost. However, one of the major limitations of this material is its random electron transport resulting in the recombination of electron-hole pair which leads to poor performance [4]. Continuous efforts are going on to improve the performance of the cell from the beginning [2]. Gao et al. has reported an efficiency of 11.1% after modifying the dye while Yella et al. [5] has obtained 12.3% using a modified electrolyte having a more facile cobalt(II/III) redox system [6]. However, the cobalt based electrolyte is environmentally not benign and also quite expensive. Many groups are trying to improve the stability of the electrolyte using gel based electrolytes and ionic liquids which are beneficial for bright sunny days [7]. In comparison, modification strategies on the anode [8–11] have not been greatly successful and the efficiency continues to remain moderate despite its prevalence and wide popularity. Hence, there is an urgent requirement for improving the anode configuration to make DSSCs more efficient and reliable for wider utilization.

Anodes could be modified using a variety of size selective quantum dots (QDs) like CdSe and CdTe in order to get beneficial size quantization effects by tuning the bandgap [12]. The primary advantage of using quantum dots in the photoanode is to harness the photons in the UV region so that they might act as co-sensitizer. However, the main drawback of using Cd based quantum dots are toxicity and the lack of long term stability. Incorporation of carbon nanostructures in TiO2 photoanode minimizes the back electron transfer to electrolyte as well as enhancement of electron transport in TiO2 which improves the overall photovoltaic parameters [13]. In recent times, carbon (1D or 2D) nanostructure modified photoanodes for DSSC applications show an increased dye adsorption, rapid electron transport and low recombination rate, hence these factors conclude the photovoltaic performance by synergistic effect between the carbon/semiconductor network [14–16]. Graphene Quantum Dots (GQDs) could be the preferred materials due to not only its low cytotoxicity, but because of the ease of functionalization and processability. Consequently, many attempts have been made in the past for the incorporation of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) in the photoanode in order to enhance the efficiency along with simultaneous improvements in the dye so that the performance of quantum dot sensitized/co-sensitized solar cell with and without dye could be compared [12,17–22]. Unfortunately, the low photoluminescence quantum yield (below 20%) of the pristine GQDs obviates its effective utilization. Yan and co-workers [23] have used large, stable, colloidal GQDs for modifying the anode, the power conversion efficiency was not very high with a short-circuit current density (Jsc) of 200μAcm−2, due to the suboptimal interaction of large GQDs and TiO2 surface. In comparison, N-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) have shown relatively better performance with rutile TiO2 where both improved open-circuit voltage (Voc of 0.46V), and JSC (of 0.69mAcm−2) could be measured with an overall conversion efficiency of 0.13% [24]. In addition to this, direct doping of TiO2 using N and S exhibited a narrow bandgap which could shift significantly towards the visible region thus enhancing the performance further [4,25,26]. Apart from this, few groups also used GQDs for modifying the counter electrodes coupled with graphene foam to display enhanced efficiency. For example, Chang et al. [27] in this way has observed an efficiency of 9.59% ascribed mainly to the electro-catalytic effect of the GQDs on graphene foam. The combination of TiO2-GQD-Dye helped in tuning the electronic levels of GQDs and also in such a cascaded configuration of energy levels that enhanced charge collection and separation were possible. Another important aspect in this case was the energy transfer from GQDs to dyes promoting the application of GQDs in solar cells due to the overlap between the luminescence of GQDs and the absorption of dye molecules. Further, Mihalache et al. has investigated that the charge and energy transfer process occurs simultaneously between GQDs and TiO2 due to the change in energy level alignment and also acts as co sensitizers [28]. The fluorescent carbon quantum dots modified TiO2 can enhance the incident photon to current efficiency (IPCE) from 70% to 96% after modification. However, the photo voltage is decreased for this modified electrode due to increase in recombination resistance resulting back electron transfer to redox species [29]. Although the electron recombination to the redox electrolyte couple was expected to be diminished because of the inhibition of the back electron transfer to the electrolyte by the GQDs, there were many unanswered questions about the role of GQDs, especially its size dependence, stability and interactions on TiO2 surface, in improving the performance of DSSCs [11,12,28]. Recently, doped GQDs exhibit unique behavior especially in tuning the optical properties and electronic band structure that utilized for optoelectronic device applications. Fluorinated GQDs have been synthesized by hydrothermal route which exhibit higher blue luminescence compared to the fluorinated 2D graphene sheets and hence it could be a potential candidate for opto electronic devices [30]. Likewise, nitrogen doped GQDs show in the form of pyrrolic and pyridinic structure which tune up the optical properties and hence it introduces new energy level for electronic device applications [31]. These improved PL enhancement in the visible region potentially applied as a co-sensitizers for DSSC applications.

Here we report an interesting strategy to modify the photo anode of DSSC using NFS-GQDs in the TiO2 meso-porous network in order to overcome the above problems and also to enhance the light harvesting efficiency with an alluring PCE value of 11.7%. More specifically, the co-doping of these selected heteroatoms has been a critical parameter to reveal astounding performance as the N doping has helped to reduce the bandgap of TiO2 while S could facilitate the electron transfer process and F in the effective binding with the TiO2 surface [4] . Thus, the performance improvement could be reflected in the standard configuration of NFS-GQDs modified anodes by a prominent shift of the visible region of the N719 dye towards the red region, indicating that more number of photons would be absorbed in the covered region. This obviously acted as a charge carrier to facilitate the accelerated electron transport and also to suppress the electron recombination rate. Interestingly, the dangling “F” from the NFS-GQDs could also possibly make hydrogen bonding with the −OH groups of the TiO2 nano-particles contributing to enhanced stability. Thus, our results illustrate the possibility of improving the next-generation DSSC anodes which could probably be coupled with more proficient counter electrodes and modified dye or altered electrolytes to achieve exceptional performance. A schematic diagram of the DSSC indicating the flat-band potential (Efb) shift in the presence of NFS-GQDs (52mV) along with other possible improvements is displayed in Scheme 1 . Some of the defining features of our modification along with the increased energy difference between the TiO2 level and the redox potential giving rise to enhancement in Voc in presence of NFS-GQDs is indicated to understand the reasons for the improved efficiency.

Fig. 1 shows a comparison of the low and high magnification electron microscopic images of NFS-GQDs incorporated on different phase of TiO2 particles (average 50nm). Fig. 1(a) shows an overall low magnification image of NFS-GQDs distribution on TiO2. Due to very small particle size, NFS-GQDs are masked by bigger TiO2 particles and difficult to distinguish in the low magnification image. However, HRTEM images (Fig. 1(b) and (c)) show clear lattice fringes of both rutile (110) and anatase (101) TiO2 particles (matches with Fig. S2) along with (002) and (100) planes of NFS-GQDs respectively corresponding to that of graphene. The figure also reveals the growth of some of the TiO2 nano particles from ∼21nm (Fig. S1) to ∼50nm in diameter due to sintering during the device fabrication process. However, the size of the GQDs are invariant (average 2nm) since the modification is carried out only after the sintering processes (also supported by HRTEM).

Fig. 2 (a) exhibits the UV-VIS-DRS spectra of the TiO2, TiO2-NFS-GQDs with and without the dye adsorption. All the NFS-GQDs modified data are related to an optimum exposure for a period of 8h in this figure which shows a better performance compared to other dipping time (UV-VIS-DRS not provided for other dipping time, I–V discussed in the later part) An absorption peak at 370nm (bandgap 3.2eV) has been observed due to anatase phase while a band edge absorption at 410nm (bandgap 3eV) indicates the presence of rutile form of TiO2 which is further bolstered by the XRD analysis and TEM SAED [32]. In the presence of NFS-GQD, there is a marginal increment in the overall intensity for TiO2 photoanode. However, TiO2- NFS-GQD-dye photoanode combination shows a slight depression in the intensity with a bathochromic shift of 20nm in the absorption onset. The overall integrated area under the absorption envelopwith and without NFS-GQDs was found to be 8% more for the NFS-GQDs modified photoanode. The enhanced dye adsorption for NFS-GQD/TiO2 photoanode may be due to the ᴨ-ᴨ and also possible Van der Waals' interaction. The bathochromic shift has been observed probably due to enhanced donor – acceptor interactions due to the presence of auxiliary electron receptor (NFS-GQDs) [18]. In order to confirm this hypothesis, a UV–vis analysis (Fig. S3) has been carried out for estimating the dye loading[17] (elaborated in the supporting information) which confirms almost the double amount of dye adsorption upon the incorporation of NFS-GQDs (0.01mM in the presence of NFS-GQDs while 0.005mM for pristine) [17]. In order to probe this further, a comparison of the UV–vis spectra of only N719 dye and the mixture of dye-doped GQDs has been carried out. Accordingly, Fig. 2(b) reveals a ∼50nm bathochromic edge shift indicating the strong affinity of the dye molecule in presence of doped GQDs. The extent of loading with time has been estimated using XPS (supporting information Fig. S8–S10). Fig. S1 is associated with the XPS of NFS-GQDs and Fig. S8 to S10 show the variation of surface modification with time of exposure to NFS-GQDs. From these figures, it can be seen that amount of Ti on surface gradually decreases with the dipping time from 8h to 12h and goes down upon incorporation of dye further. Details regarding XPS analysis has been given in the supporting information.

Photolumiescent Spectroscopy (PL) is a powerful tool to analyse trapping, migration and charge carrier transfer associated with quantum dots since PL emissions normally come from radiative electron-hole pair recombination of free carriers. Accordingly, Fig. 2(c) reveals the superimposed PL spectra of NFS-GQDs,TiO2 and TiO2-NFS-GQDs. The modified photoanode shows a depressed PL emission intensity in the region 400–500nm and a broad peak centered at 430nm compared to that for TiO2 anode (with an excitation wavelength of 370nm). A significantly lower PL intensity indicates that perhaps the added NFS-GQDS have reduced the charge traps in the TiO2 film facilitating transfer in an effective manner. The lower intensity indicates the quenching effect of NFS-GQDs as evidenced by the charge transfer between TiO2 and NFS-GQDs due to the tuning of the Fermi-level alignment.

Fig. 3 (a) depicts the I–V (current-voltage) characteristics of the cells measured under 1 sun (100mW/cm2) illumination with AM 1.5G. It compares the influence of NFS-GQDs on the device performance as a function of the loading (indirectly estimated from a systematic control of the time of dipping viz., 3,8 and 12h) A remarkable improvement in PCE is observed for the case of 8h NFS-GQD modification. There is increment of 47mV in the open circuit voltage (Voc). Importantly, the short circuit current density (Jsc) (22.6mA/cm2) and fill factor (FF) (71%) with respect to TiO2-dye anodes (15.75mA/cm2, 63%) Table 1 . The reasons for this significant improvement after the incorporation NFS-GQDs (11.7%) could be attributed to multiple factors like enhanced absorption of photons enabling more photocurrent, functioning like a co-sensitizer and also improving the electronic properties at the same time as NFS-GQDs treated TiO2 can effectively tune the Fermi level of TiO2 to negative side resulting in higher Voc values [33,34]. Indeed, many studies [4,25], reveal the origin of the enhanced absorption to be mainly because of the dopants in the GQDs, where the existence of S facilitates the electron transfer process concomitantly, increasing the forward reduction reaction. Furthermore, the modification by GQDs can also change the rate of recombination favorably to facilitate better performance of the cell. However, when loading increases or decreases beyond a certain limit, the efficiency degrades. The probable reason for this could be the enhanced resistance of the device due to higher loading. The S shaped J-V curve for the 12h case indicates power loss due to the parasitic effects arise for the increased Rs which has been observed clearly in the impedance data. Rs is inversely proportional to the slope of the J-V curve. More loading of NFS-GQDs indicate the increased resistance of the system and also lower amount (0.2 times lower for 8h than 12h) of dye absorption (Table S1) which eventually leads to poor performance of the cell.

As a proof of this concept, a device has been fabricated with NFS-GQD as a sensitizer without dye which showed an efficiency of 0.71% and FF=53% (8h sensitized NFS-GQD) (Fig. S5). This value is comparable with that of conventional QD sensitized solar cell and even better than that of GQDs sensitized data reported recently (0.1%) [20,28]. For a comparison, (with and without doping with NFS-GQDs), a cell fabricated with the same sized GQDs without any doping shows only an efficiency of ∼9.1% (data has not been provided here for brevity). The interfacial charge transfer process could be monitored through electrochemical impedance study (EIS) [35]. Fig. 3(b) shows the corresponding impedance data indicating a comparison of the internal resistance (summarized in Table 2 ) for different loading of NFS-GQDs using Bisquert element fitting (Fig. S5). The impedance has been performed under illumination with a bias potential range from −1 to 1V within a frequency region of 1Hz to 100kHz. The detail circuit of transmission model line equivalent circuit is fitted for DSSC impedance spectra (Fig. S4). Table 2 includes all the important parameters derived from impedance data. The elements used for fitting is described as follows. Rs is the overall resistance of device. The first semicircle represents: Rpt − charge transfer resistance between counter electrode and electrolyte, the second semicircle: Rct1 − charge transfer resistance between photoanode and electrolyte and the third semicircle: Rct2 − charge transfer resistance between photoanode and FTO. CPE- capacitance at counter electrode, Wd − diffusion resistance of electrolyte, the second semicircle represents the recombination resistance at Photoanode and electrolyte interface (Rr) Mg- anomalous diffusion or Bisquert element. Wd denotes diffusion resistance of electrolyte. The anomalous diffusion element is used for fitting to understand the recombination resistance between photoanode and electrolyte. Comparing with the unmodified photoanode, modified photoanode with GQDs shows higher Rr that enhances the PCE of DSSCs.

The device with NFS-GQDs (8h) modified TiO2 has shown a higher Rr value of 9.9ohm compared to that for pristine TiO2 (1.9ohm). This higher Rr indicates better performance in terms of recombination process. The recombination electron life time for the 8h case and TiO2 has been calculated from the Bode plot also to understand the reasons for improvement. Interestingly, in Fig. 3(c), the frequency in the Bode plot has been shifted to the lower side for NFS-GQD/TiO2 (8h) compared to that for bare TiO2, 3h and 12h modified cases. The lower the frequency in the Bode plot, the higher is the electron life time [4]. More electron lifetime indicates less recombination thus giving a better performance. Electron life time can be calculated from the maximum frequency point in Bode plot, Tn =(1/2ᴨf), where f is the maximum frequency as [4] summarized in Table 2. For NFS-GQD (9.57ms) longer electron lifetime is seen compared to that for pure TiO2 (6.5ms) revealing that the doped-GQD prevents the back electron transfer from TiO2 more effectively. Furthermore, to support the high PCE observed in this case, the steady state electron density was also calculated as in the supporting information [7,36]. The device with higher ns, obviously shows better photovoltaic performance. More specifically, a higher ns value of 3.64 *1018 cm−3 compared to that for unmodified device (1.99 *1018 cm−3) has been obtained from the Mott-Schottky (MS) (Fig. 3(d)) analysis indicating also the added doped-GQD shifts the flat-band potential (Efb) of TiO2 to the negative potential. Hence, the difference between the Ef and the redox potential is increased [9] resulting a higher Voc. In addition, upon the NFS-GQD modification a corresponding improvement is also manifested in the FF value. The donor density for the TiO2/NFS-GQDs/dye interface could be extracted from the right hand side slope of the MS analysis (Fig. S6) using the following formula.
ND =(2/e€€0)[d(1/C2)/dV]−1,
where e=elementary charge, €0 =permittivity of vacuum, €=permittivity of semiconductor and d(1/C2)/dV=slope obtained from MS plot. Accordingly, the donor density is seen to increase with a decrease in the slope indicating an enhanced donor density. A negative shift of the flat band potential for the 3h (−0.239V) and 8h (−0.289V) case compared to that for the bare TiO2 (−0.234V) indicates a shift in the upward direction of the Fermi level. However, upon increasing the concentration of doped-GQDs further, the Fermi level shifts downwards which could be seen in the increased potential value at −0.206V and thus rendering poor Voc as seen in Fig. 3(a). A significant shift in flat band potential (52 mv) has been observed in this case compared to that in the earlier reports on Fe doped TiO2 (18mV) [9] or perovskite solar cells (37mV) which could be attributed to the charge localization by Fermi level pinning which causes perhaps a decreased work function of TiO2 [37]. Earlier reports demonstrate that GQDs can enhance the efficiency of DSSC and the size distribution of GQDs plays an important role in this, Indeed, as the size decreases, Voc increases slightly despite a decrease in Jsc due to the enhanced hetero-junction and thus increased hole transportation barrier [38]. However, an increased value for both the Voc and Jsc have been observed in this study by using average 2nm NFS-GQDs. More importantly these values are found to be stable for few weeks (about four weeks) with neither visible degradation nor agglomeration suggesting a stable GQDs distribution.

Furthermore, IPCE analyses (Fig. 4 ) of the DSSC cell with and without NFS-GQD shows a remarkably enhanced IPCE (88%) in the visible region (520nm) for modified photoanode and an additional improvement has been observed between (400–450nm) and upto 600nm also as it functions as a co-sensitizer. TiO2 − NFS-GQD combination is efficient in the UV region too. The Jsc calculated from J-V (22.6mA/cm2) for TiO2 – NFS-GQD matches with the Jsc calculated from IPCE (average 22.01mA/cm2) by integration of the IPCE curve area. Similarly, for blank system (TiO2 only) it has shown a average value of 15.3mA/cm2 (by IPCE area integration) and 15.75mA/cm2 by J-V experiment. The formula used for Jsc plot is:

IPCE (λ) %=[1240*Jsc (mA/cm2)]*100/[λ (nm)*P (mW/cm2)]; where, λ is the wavelength, P=incident irradiation flux and other terms hold the usual meaning as explained before. This shows improved in Jsc due to excellent charge collection and transport probably due to the FRET (fluorescence resonance energy transfer) mechanism between the dye molecule and NFS-GQDs which amplifies the IPCE in the visible region [28]. Fig. S12 shows the overlapping of TiO2 +N719 dye absorbance and PL of NFS-GQDs spectra which reveals the possibility of FRET type charge transfer. In addition to this, the decay time measurements (Fig. S13) of NFS-GQDs reveals higher average life time (2.42ns) with respect to pristine undoped GQDs (1.58ns). These results indicate that the doping helps in the lifetime enhancement of the electron which could be one potential reason for low recombination in case of doped GQDs compared to undoped GQDs.

These electrochemical results are also in good agreement with all other supporting data like UV-VIS, IR, IPCE, PL and TCSPC. We attribute that the Voc enhancement of the modified doped GQDs is mainly due to the Efb shift and photocurrent enhancement due to the increased donor density upon modification as an optimum concentration of NFS-GQDs provide all benefits to support enhanced PCE with a good fill factor.
Synthesis of NFS-GQDs has been reported from our research group already [39]. DSSC fabrication has been provided in the supporting information.

In summary, our findings with respect to the modification of TiO2 photoanodes using co-doped GQDs provide important insights for the reengineering of DSSCs with improved performance. A higher value of power conversion efficiency (11.7%) could be realized with excellent FF (functional area=0.16cm2) for these DSSCs since the presence of doped GQDs helps to prevent the back electron transfer from TiO2 in addition to enhanced absorption features. Interestingly, it has shown an efficiency around 10% even after one month (after refilling of electrolyte) thus manifesting the stability. Apart from the fundamental relevance of improving the performance of QD sensitized solar cells, these results also suggest the important advantages of using an environment-friendly material unlike conventional QDs. We hope some more engineering in the fabrication of the cell as well in the material will lead to next-generation, eco-friendly solar cells with more conversion efficiency in the near future.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Authors acknowledge the financial support by CSIR under TAPSUN program (FLEXISOLAR [NWP-54]). Authors also acknowledge IUSSTF joint center for the student exchange program on the project “3D engineered electrodes for electrochemical energy storage” through grant number 22-2012/2013-14 and its members Prof. P. M. Ajayan, Prof. R. Vajtai and Prof. S. Talapatra. SK acknowledges CSIR for SRF fellowship. Authors thank Mr. Rathish Kumar, Ms. Panjali, Ms. Nalini and all other CIF staffs, CSIR-CECRI for their help rendered during the characterization of the samples. Authors thank Dr. Ravichandran for his help and support.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.electacta.2017.05.024

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: World Energy Scenarios: Composing energy futures to 2050
A. low-cost, high-efficiency solar cell based on dye-sensitized colloidal TiO2 films
Nature
Graphene quantum-dot-doped polypyrrole counter electrode for high-performance dye-sensitized solar cells
ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces.
Boosting Photovoltaic Performance of Dye-Sensitized Solar Cells Using Silver Nanoparticle-Decorated N, S-Co-Doped-TiO2 Photoanode
Sci. Rep.
A new heteroleptic ruthenium sensitizer enhances the absorptivity of mesoporous titania film for a high efficiency dye-sensitized solar cell
Chem. Commun.
Porphyrin-Sensitized Solar Cells with Cobalt (II/III)-Based Redox Electrolyte Exceed 12 Percent Efficiency
Science (80-.)
Fabrication of Stable Dye Sensitized Solar Cell with Gel electrolytes Using Poly(ethylene oxide)-Poly(ethylene glycol)
Electrochim. Acta.
Dye-sensitized solar cells with 13% efficiency achieved through the molecular engineering of porphyrin sensitizers
Nat. Chem.
